items.question_text: If you become aware that an aircraft is aligned with the wrong surface on approach, what is the required response?

choices.text: Inform the pilot and issue appropriate control instructions or clearances, including a go-around if necessary.
Wait for the pilot to notice and correct the alignment independently.
Issue landing clearance for the intended runway and assume the pilot will realign on short final.
Transfer the aircraft to ground control so they can verify the runway surface.

items.explanation: If the aircraft is aligned with the wrong surface, the pilot must be informed and given the necessary control instructions or clearances.

items.instruction: Which transmission is required if the hold-short instructions are not read back with the landing clearance?
items.question_text: Which transmission is required?

choices.text: READ BACK HOLD SHORT INSTRUCTIONS.
VERIFY SHORT RUNWAY ACCEPTANCE.
REPORT CLEAR OF THE HOLD LINE.
CANCEL LAND AND HOLD SHORT.

items.explanation: If the hold-short instructions are not read back with the landing clearance, the controller must request the readback.

items.question_text: Whenever the landing runway is changed, you must preface the landing clearance with change to runway followed by the runway number, then restate the runway number with the landing clearance.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The runway-change phraseology must preface the revised landing clearance and the runway number must be restated.

items.question_text: Inform the pilot and issue control instructions or clearances if you become aware the aircraft is aligned with the wrong surface.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That is the required response when wrong-surface alignment is detected.

items.instruction: Is this same-runway LUAW landing-clearance rule correct?
items.question_text: If a facility does not have a safety logic system, or the system is inoperative or in a limited configuration, you must not clear an aircraft for a full-stop, touch-and-go, stop-and-go, low approach, or option on the same runway with an aircraft holding in position or taxiing to LUAW until the aircraft in position has exited the runway or starts takeoff roll.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Without the full safety-logic authorization, same-runway landing clearances cannot be issued over traffic holding in position or taxiing to LUAW until the stated condition exists.

items.situation: The pilot expected Runway 27 Left, but you need to land the aircraft on Runway 27 Right. Build the landing clearance.
items.target_phrase: CHANGE TO RUNWAY TWO SEVEN RIGHT RUNWAY TWO SEVEN RIGHT CLEARED TO LAND

items.situation: Your tower does not have a safety logic system in service. An aircraft is holding in position on the runway, and another aircraft on final is requesting a full-stop landing. Another controller says you can still clear the arrival to land now because the aircraft in position should begin takeoff shortly and the arrival pilot can see the traffic.
items.para_context: Without a safety logic system, or with it inoperative or limited, a full-stop, touch-and-go, stop-and-go, low approach, or option may not be cleared on the same runway with an aircraft holding in position or taxiing to LUAW until the aircraft in position has exited the runway or starts takeoff roll.

choices.text: Do not clear the arrival to land yet; wait until the aircraft in position exits the runway or starts takeoff roll.
Clear the arrival to land now because visual acquisition by the arriving pilot replaces the safety-logic restriction.
Issue the landing clearance now as long as the aircraft in position reads back ready for immediate takeoff.
Use a change-to-runway clearance instead of a landing clearance to avoid the restriction.

items.explanation: Without the authorized safety-logic case, the landing clearance must wait until the aircraft holding in position exits the runway or starts takeoff roll.

items.question_text: When may you anticipate landing separation for a succeeding aircraft in sequence?

choices.text: When you observe the aircraft positions and determine prescribed runway separation will exist when the aircraft crosses the landing threshold.
Whenever the runway is clear of departing traffic when the second aircraft first checks in.
Whenever the succeeding pilot says they are comfortable following close behind the preceding arrival.
Only when LUAW operations are in use on the same runway.

items.explanation: Anticipated landing separation is allowed when the controller observes the positions and determines the required runway separation will exist at threshold crossing.

items.instruction: Which situation requires traffic information to the succeeding aircraft when using anticipated landing separation?
items.question_text: Which situation requires traffic information?

choices.text: Traffic will be departing prior to the succeeding aircraft's arrival.
The preceding arrival was previously reported and remains in plain view on the same final.
The runway has not yet been changed to the arrival runway.
The pilot requested a full-stop instead of the option.

items.explanation: Traffic information must be issued when a preceding arrival has not been previously reported and when traffic will be departing prior to the arrival.

items.instruction: Is this anticipated-separation traffic-information requirement correct?
items.question_text: When using anticipated landing separation, you must issue traffic information to the succeeding aircraft if a preceding arrival has not been previously reported and when traffic will be departing prior to the arrival.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Those are the required traffic-information cases in the paragraph.

items.question_text: If you observe the positions of aircraft in a landing sequence and determine prescribed runway separation will exist when the succeeding aircraft crosses the landing threshold, you may issue the landing clearance before full separation already exists.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The landing clearance need not be withheld if the controller can determine the required separation will exist at threshold crossing.

items.question_text: This anticipated-separation exception applies only in the authorized safety-logic LUAW case, not in ordinary LUAW operations.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The paragraph prohibits use with LUAW except for the specifically authorized case.

items.situation: The arrival can be cleared to land, and another aircraft will depart before it reaches the runway. Build the transmission.
items.target_phrase: RUNWAY ONE EIGHT CLEARED TO LAND TRAFFIC WILL DEPART PRIOR TO YOUR ARRIVAL

items.situation: Your facility is using full core alert runway safety logic, reported weather is above 800 feet and 2 miles, and an arrival is on final with traffic holding in position on the same runway. You can observe the aircraft positions and determine prescribed runway separation will exist when the arrival reaches the threshold. Another controller says anticipating separation can never be used once LUAW is involved.
items.para_context: Anticipating separation is generally not used during LUAW operations, except in the authorized safety-logic case. When using the provision, applicable traffic information must still be issued.

choices.text: You may use anticipated landing separation in this authorized safety-logic case, but you must issue the applicable traffic information.
You may never use anticipated landing separation once LUAW is involved, regardless of safety logic configuration or weather.
You may use anticipated landing separation only if you omit the traffic information to avoid distracting the arrival pilot.
You may use anticipated landing separation only after the aircraft in position has already started takeoff roll, because the authorization never applies to holding-in-position traffic.

items.explanation: The authorized safety-logic case is the exception to the LUAW prohibition, but the required traffic information still must be issued.

items.question_text: What must be stated first when issuing a landing clearance?
items.explanation: The runway number is stated first, followed by the landing clearance.

choices.text: The runway number.
The wind.
The traffic information.
The aircraft call sign.

items.question_text: What must be requested if hold-short instructions are not read back with the landing clearance?
items.explanation: If the hold-short instructions are not read back, the controller must request the readback.

choices.text: Read back hold short instructions.
Verify runway acceptance.
Report clear of the hold line.
Cancel land and hold short.

items.question_text: What phrase must preface a landing clearance when the landing runway is changed?
items.explanation: The landing clearance must be prefaced with change to runway followed by the new runway number.

choices.text: Change to runway.
Runway revised.
Use runway.
Expect runway.

items.question_text: If you become aware an aircraft is aligned with the wrong surface, what must you do?
items.explanation: The pilot must be informed and given control instructions or clearances as needed.

choices.text: Inform the pilot and issue control instructions or clearances.
Wait for the pilot to recognize the problem.
Transfer the aircraft to approach for runway verification.
Issue landing clearance for the intended runway and monitor the correction.

items.question_text: Without a safety logic system, or with it inoperative or limited, when may an arrival be cleared for a full-stop, touch-and-go, stop-and-go, low approach, or option on the same runway with an aircraft holding in position or taxiing to LUAW?
items.explanation: Without the authorized safety-logic case, the aircraft in position must exit the runway or start takeoff roll first.

choices.text: Only after the aircraft in position has exited the runway or starts takeoff roll.
As soon as the arriving pilot reports the traffic in sight.
As soon as the aircraft in position reads back ready for immediate departure.
Any time weather is at or above 800 feet and 2 miles.

items.question_text: What word must follow the runway number when runway length has been temporarily or permanently shortened in a landing clearance?
items.explanation: The word shortened must be stated as part of the landing clearance.

choices.text: Shortened.
Reduced.
Construction.
Temporary.

items.question_text: When may a landing clearance to a succeeding aircraft in sequence be issued before full runway separation already exists?
items.explanation: The controller may anticipate landing separation if observed positions show the required separation will exist at threshold crossing.

choices.text: When observed aircraft positions show prescribed runway separation will exist when the aircraft crosses the landing threshold.
Whenever the preceding aircraft has touched down.
Whenever the runway is expected to be clear by the time the arrival reaches midfield.
Only when the arrival sequence number is issued.

items.question_text: When must traffic information be issued to the succeeding aircraft under this paragraph?
items.explanation: Traffic information is required if a preceding arrival has not been previously reported and when traffic will depart prior to the arrival.

choices.text: If a preceding arrival has not been previously reported and when traffic will depart prior to the arrival.
Only when a preceding arrival is more than 5 miles from touchdown.
Only when the succeeding aircraft asks for traffic advisories.
Only when a departing aircraft is on the tower frequency but still holding short.

items.question_text: What phrase may be included when traffic will depart before the arriving aircraft reaches the runway?
items.explanation: The example phraseology says traffic will depart prior to your arrival.

choices.text: Traffic will depart prior to your arrival.
Traffic will be gone by the numbers.
Traffic will roll after your touchdown.
Traffic is expected off the runway soon.

items.question_text: May anticipated landing separation be applied during LUAW operations in all cases?
items.explanation: No. It must not be applied during LUAW operations except in the specifically authorized case.

choices.text: No, except for the specifically authorized safety-logic case.
Yes, as long as the arrival pilot sees the aircraft in position.
Yes, but only if the tower omits traffic information.
No, never under any circumstances.

items.question_text: What is the note about landing sequence numbers at tower facilities where approach control establishes the arrival sequence to the runway?
items.explanation: The landing sequence number is optional when approach control has already established the runway sequence.

choices.text: The landing sequence number is optional.
The landing sequence number is mandatory.
The landing sequence number may be used only for VFR arrivals.
The landing sequence number is replaced by the wake category.

items.question_text: When using the authorized LUAW exception to anticipated landing separation, what must still be issued?
items.explanation: Applicable traffic information still must be issued when that provision is used.

choices.text: Applicable traffic information.
A runway-change advisory.
A mandatory landing sequence number.
A wake-turbulence waiver acknowledgment.
